background: Pixels outside all ventricular cavities and outside the myocardial ring, without enclosed bright blood regions.
Outer field around the heart, showing soft tissue or fat without any chamber-like closed contour.
Regions beyond the epicardial border with heterogeneous texture, not resembling a smooth cavity interior.
Areas where no continuous wall surrounds a lumen, lacking any ring-like or crescent-shaped structure.
Non-cardiac surroundings with irregular shapes, not centrally located within the image frame.
Peripheral tissue bands along the image edges, without adjacency to a bright ventricular cavity.
Static background regions that keep similar appearance across frames, unlike changing ventricular volumes.
Areas with mixed intensities and striated texture, consistent with muscles rather than blood pool.
Dark or speckled regions away from the heart, without a sharp inner boundary of a chamber.
Outside-heart pixels separated from cavities by the outer myocardial contour, not part of any lumen.
Background zones lacking a smooth closed curve, with no enclosed homogeneous bright signal region.
Non-chamber regions where boundaries are diffuse and do not form continuous inner and outer edges.
Pixels lateral to the heart silhouette, not touching the myocardial wall or any ventricular cavity.
Regions inferior or superior to the ventricles where no circular cavity appears in short-axis view.
Areas containing spine or posterior structures, showing complex texture and no cavity-like uniformity.
Chest wall or subcutaneous areas with layered appearance, not surrounded by a myocardial wall.
Non-cardiac tissue with streaks and fibers, not showing homogeneous blood-like intensity.
Corners of the field-of-view with no heart anatomy, lacking any ring-shaped segmentation target.
Background pixels that do not participate in systolic shrinkage or diastolic expansion patterns.
Areas outside both ventricular lumens and myocardium, forming the remainder of the image domain.
Regions where intensity is inconsistent within small patches, unlike the relatively uniform cavity interior.
Pixels separated from the heart by clear outer borders, not located inside any myocardial enclosure.
Non-heart regions without adjacent myocardial thickness changes between systole and diastole frames.
Areas with thin, jagged edges and no continuous contour enclosing a lumen-like region.
Regions far from the central cardiac structures, showing no crescent or elliptical cavity geometry.
Background tissue lacking a smooth endocardial-like boundary, with irregular gradients and textures.
Pixels outside all short-axis ventricular cross-sections, not forming part of any central cavity.
Non-cardiac structures that remain visually stationary, unlike moving and deforming ventricular areas.
Areas without any paired inner and outer boundaries characteristic of the myocardial wall.
Regions with coarse texture and no enclosed bright pool, consistent with surrounding anatomy.
Pixels outside the myocardial outer edge, not contained within the heart-shaped region of interest.
Non-lumen regions where intensity resembles general tissue, not the homogeneous appearance of blood pool.
Areas showing no chamber adjacency, not located between myocardium and an internal bright cavity.
Background zones with fragmented edges, not matching the smooth continuous contours of ventricles.
Non-cardiac pixels that do not form a closed contour even when viewed across consecutive frames.
Peripheral regions with no central cavity, lacking the typical ventricular cross-sectional structures.
Areas with variable brightness and no consistent boundary, not compatible with ventricular lumen segmentation.
Pixels outside the heart where contours are absent, and anatomy does not show ring-like topology.
Regions that are not enclosed by myocardium and do not lie within any ventricular interior.
Background parts that maintain shape across the cardiac cycle, unlike cavities and wall thickening.
right_ventricle: Crescent-shaped bright cavity with a curved, banana-like interior, typically smaller than the central left cavity.
Irregular lumen with a thin crescent geometry, not perfectly round, located near the anterior side.
Bright blood pool forming a partial arc, with concave inner edge and more variable outline than LV.
Small, asymmetric cavity that appears as a curved sliver adjacent to the myocardial wall.
Non-circular lumen often shaped like a crescent or wedge, with uneven thickness of surrounding wall.
Bright region with a curved topology, sometimes split or tapered across slices near the apex.
Right-sided chamber interior with a crescent contour, differing from the larger central elliptical cavity.
Anterior or anterolateral bright pool with irregular perimeter, not fully symmetric around its center.
Cavity with a curved, open-ring appearance, often wrapping partly around the LV outer side.
Smaller bright lumen that may appear elongated, with a narrower cross-section than the LV pool.
Region inside myocardium where the lumen looks like a crescent, not a closed round disk.
Bright pool adjacent to LV myocardium, separated by a wall, with noticeably less regular outline.
Chamber interior located closer to the chest wall side, often offset from the image center.
Lumen positioned near the front of the heart cross-section, adjacent to background on the anterior edge.
Cavity that sits next to the LV but with different geometry, typically more crescent-like than circular.
Bright region neighboring the septal wall, lying outside the LV ring but inside its own boundary.
Non-central lumen with an arc-like shape, often touching myocardium on multiple sides.
Crescent cavity located beside the LV, sharing proximity yet remaining a separate enclosed lumen.
Right ventricular blood pool positioned near the myocardial ring, but not the central enclosed cavity.
Bright crescent that appears on one side of the heart, adjacent to the LV outer boundary region.
Lumen with a relatively sharp inner boundary, but an irregular outer border defined by myocardium.
Cavity interior is relatively homogeneous and bright, bounded by a thinner, curved myocardial layer.
Endocardial boundary appears curved and sometimes less smooth, forming a tapered crescent outline.
Bright pool with a jagged or irregular contour compared with LV, especially in mid-ventricular slices.
RV lumen often shows less circularity, with corners or points where the crescent narrows.
Homogeneous bright interior with a boundary that may look less continuous in very small RV slices.
Cavity region with a thin, curved shape and moderate boundary sharpness against surrounding myocardium.
Bright pool whose border can appear slightly fragmented when the RV is very small in systole.
Interior texture is uniform, unlike surrounding muscle, and the boundary traces a curved crescent.
Right-side blood pool with variable shape across slices, maintaining a bright, cavity-like appearance.
In diastole the crescent lumen expands, becoming thicker and more open along the curved arc.
In systole the RV lumen contracts substantially, sometimes becoming a narrow curved sliver.
Across the cardiac cycle the RV area changes, while maintaining a non-circular, crescent geometry.
RV cavity size reduces in systole, with myocardium moving inward and the crescent becoming smaller.
Diastolic frames show a larger RV pool, with smoother curvature and less tapering at the ends.
Systolic frames show increased wall proximity, making the RV lumen thinner and more irregular.
Between diastole and systole the RV outline shifts and shrinks, often more noticeably than LV.
Late systole may show a tiny RV lumen near the anterior edge, still brighter than myocardium.
Diastole can reveal a broader RV crescent adjacent to LV, with clearer lumen continuity.
RV blood pool exhibits strong area change over time, while keeping its anterior, crescent placement.
myocardium: Ring-like muscular wall surrounding the left ventricular cavity, forming a closed or near-closed annulus.
Thick curved band encircling the central bright LV lumen, with consistent wall topology across the slice.
Continuous wall structure between bright blood pool and outer tissues, forming an inner and outer boundary.
Curved muscular layer that outlines the LV cavity, appearing as a donut-like ring in short-axis view.
Annular wall with variable thickness, separating the central cavity from the surrounding background.
Myocardial tissue forming a circular or elliptical ring around the LV, sometimes with minor gaps near base.
Wall segment that partially wraps around the RV region, but most clearly forms the LV circumferential ring.
Concentric structure around the LV lumen, creating a clear topology of inside cavity and outside tissues.
Muscular band with a closed-loop appearance, defining the ventricular wall rather than the cavity interior.
Thickened ring that maintains continuity, enclosing the LV blood pool with a smooth inner contour.
Wall located between LV blood pool and outer anatomy, often appearing as a mid-intensity band.
Tissue immediately outside the LV lumen, forming the myocardial layer rather than the bright cavity.
Structure adjacent to both LV and RV lumens, separating them where they are closest in the slice.
Ring positioned centrally, surrounding the LV cavity and lying medial to surrounding background tissues.
Myocardium forms the boundary between the central LV pool and more peripheral structures in the field.
Septal wall region located between LV and RV lumens, connecting ring segments across the short axis.
Muscular layer that encloses LV, with RV appearing adjacent but outside the LV myocardial ring.
Band-like tissue that borders the LV and also neighbors the RV near the interventricular septum.
Tissue with inner edge adjacent to bright blood pool and outer edge adjacent to darker background.
Myocardial ring encircling LV, with RV lumen appearing nearby but not enclosed by the same ring.
Inner boundary of myocardium is relatively sharp against bright blood pool, tracing a smooth contour.
Outer boundary of myocardium separates muscle from surrounding tissue, often slightly less sharp than inner edge.
Wall signal is relatively uniform compared with surrounding background, distinct from the homogeneous blood pool.
Myocardium appears intermediate intensity, darker than blood pool but brighter than many background regions.
Continuous muscular texture with subtle striations, forming a coherent ring rather than a flat region.
Two-sided boundary: bright cavity on the inside and less bright tissue on the outside of the wall.
Myocardial band shows consistent continuity around LV, with minimal breaks in mid-ventricular slices.
Wall region has a smooth inner contour and a slightly rougher outer contour, matching muscle boundaries.
Compared with blood pool, myocardium is less bright and more textured, forming a clear wall band.
Myocardial pixels form a thin-to-thick ring, not a uniform bright interior like ventricular cavities.
In systole the myocardial ring thickens, reducing the LV lumen area while wall width increases.
In diastole the myocardial wall appears thinner, with the LV cavity expanding inside the ring.
Wall thickening is visible over time, with inward motion of the inner boundary during contraction.
Systolic frames show a smaller LV lumen and a thicker myocardial band encircling it.
Diastolic frames show a larger central cavity and a comparatively thinner circumferential wall.
Across frames the myocardial contour moves, but the ring topology around LV remains consistent.
Myocardial thickness changes with time, while the inner boundary stays smooth and continuous.
During contraction the wall becomes more compact and prominent, narrowing the space of the blood pool.
Relaxation phase shows reduced wall thickness, with the annular band becoming slimmer around the LV.
Temporal pattern: wall thickens when cavity shrinks, maintaining a continuous ring-like structure.
left_ventricle: Central bright cavity with round to elliptical shape, typically the most regular lumen in the slice.
Large, smooth-edged blood pool appearing as a circular or oval region near the image center.
Homogeneous bright interior forming a well-defined cavity, enclosed by a continuous myocardial ring.
Regularly shaped lumen with near-symmetric contour, often larger than the adjacent RV crescent.
Bright central cavity that looks like a disk or ellipse, with smooth curvature along its boundary.
Closed, roundish chamber interior that remains centrally located across neighboring slices.
Lumen with high uniformity inside, forming the main central cavity in short-axis cine MRI.
Elliptical or circular bright pool, clearly enclosed by myocardium and distinct from background textures.
Central cavity with stable, smooth topology, not banana-shaped and not highly irregular.
LV lumen often appears as the largest enclosed bright region, with a consistent rounded geometry.
Blood pool located inside the myocardial ring, centrally positioned compared with other structures.
Cavity adjacent to myocardium on all sides, with RV lumen appearing off-center and beside it.
LV lumen sits within a complete wall ring, while RV appears as a neighboring crescent outside the LV ring.
Central bright pool separated from background by myocardium, not directly touching image borders.
Lumen positioned near the middle of the heart cross-section, enclosed by a circumferential wall.
LV cavity often lies medial to the RV crescent, with septal wall between the two lumens.
Bright cavity surrounded by myocardium, with outer tissues beyond the wall forming the background.
Region inside the myocardial annulus, distinct from the RV location nearer the anterior side.
LV blood pool appears centrally, with myocardium forming a ring separating it from surrounding tissues.
The cavity interior is enclosed and central, unlike peripheral structures that lack a closed boundary.
Endocardial boundary is smooth and continuous, tracing a clean circle or ellipse around bright blood pool.
LV lumen shows a sharp edge against myocardium, with minimal irregularities along the contour.
Interior texture is homogeneous and bright, contrasting with the more textured myocardial wall.
The cavity boundary is continuous and rounded, producing a clear separation from surrounding muscle.
Bright pool has uniform intensity with few internal patterns, unlike muscles with striations.
LV lumen edge remains smooth across most frames, maintaining a consistent, well-defined contour.
Compared to RV, LV border is more regular and less crescent-like, with higher circularity.
The central cavity has clear boundary definition, forming a stable closed region across time frames.
Uniform bright interior with minimal gradients, bounded by myocardium that is less bright and more textured.
Lumen appears as a coherent bright region, not fragmented, with an easily traceable endocardial contour.
In diastole the LV cavity expands, appearing larger and more open within the myocardial ring.
In systole the LV cavity shrinks, with the bright central area reducing noticeably in size.
Across systole the LV blood pool becomes smaller while the surrounding wall thickens inward.
Diastolic frames show a larger circular or elliptical LV region, occupying more of the central area.
Systolic frames show reduced cavity area, with a tighter, smaller bright region at the center.
Temporal change: LV volume decreases during contraction, while maintaining a smooth rounded boundary.
During relaxation the LV lumen enlarges again, restoring a broader central bright cavity.
The LV boundary moves inward in systole and outward in diastole, keeping a continuous contour.
Cycle pattern: larger central cavity in diastole, smaller cavity in systole, with consistent enclosure by myocardium.
LV lumen size varies over time but remains the most regular, centrally located bright chamber region.
